The next thing we want to do in our Storybrand One page is include calls to action. And there are two kinds of calls to action you want to consider. The first kind of call to action is a direct call to action. This is very simple. It's something like buy now or schedule an appointment or call us today or register today or anything that gets the customer to actually take action preferably to buy your customer's product or service. That'll be pretty easy for you to fill out in a Storybrand One page. The other call to action is a transitional call to action. The transitional call to action kind of works like this. If you're not ready to do business with us, don't go away, download this PDF or watch this webinar or attend one of our live events. It continues the relationship with the customer. Now we're going to teach you in a later module on how to create some of these transitional calls to action. But in your Storybrand One page, this is where you place it. An example of a transitional call to action, let's say in the same vein of this used car dealership, might be a PDF that is called something like 10 Things to Make Sure You Look For in a Used Car. Well, I'm not ready to buy a car, but I do download this PDF and in exchange for that PDF I give you my email address. So now you have the email address of a qualified customer who's thinking about buying a car. That is really valuable to your company or to whoever you're working for. So you want to create that transitional call to action that lets people take the next step still without risk. They still haven't bought anything. It kind of works like a one-two punch of, will you marry me? No. Will you go out with me? Yeah, I'll go out with you. Will you marry me? No. Will you go out with me? Yes. Will you marry me? No. Will you marry me? No. Will you marry me? No. Will you marry me? No. Will you marry me? No. Will you marry me? Well, finally, our last two points of the Storybrand One page have to deal with the stakes in the game. What can be won or lost if customers don't do business with us or with one of our clients? And we need to define what success looks like in their life if they do business with us and what failure looks like in their life if they do not do business with us. Now, this is pretty simple, but you just take in what the customer wanted and what does life look like if they got what they wanted, if they delivered what they wanted. They're driving around in a beautiful new car. They feel great that they got a great deal. They know that they didn't get ripped off. Or in the failure category, they bought a lemon from somebody else. They think they might have gotten ripped off. They don't trust the car salesman that they had to deal with and they had a terrible experience. If there's not something to be won or lost in the story, there is no story. So those are the basics of filling out a story brand one page. Now let's look at this real quick and how it works in story. And then we're going to talk about how it works in branding. In story, you've got somebody like Katniss in Hunger Games, right? She wants to survive the Hunger Games. Externally, of course, she's got to survive. She's got to live through this thing. Internally, she's trying to stay good and authentic. And yet she's got to kill people. So there's contrast. And philosophically, we're dealing with tyranny versus democracy. Into this mix comes Heymitch, Woody from Cheers, right? He's won the Hunger Games before, so he has authority. And yet he has compassion and empathy for her position. And so he has the one-two punch that the guy needs of empathy and authority. Heymitch's plan for Katniss is that she must endear the public in order to get sponsors. And of course, she's called to action. She has to compete. And we know if she does win the Hunger Games, she survives. And District 12 rejoices. But if she loses, she dies. And District 12 is crushed. You can see these are the basic components of a story. And it's the basic components also of a story brand one page. So as we clarify our message or our client's message, we want to ask these questions and populate a story brand one sheet with the answers. First, what does our customer want? Then what are their external, internal, and philosophical problems? Have we demonstrated empathy and authority? Have we demonstrated competence? Have we demonstrated that we care? Have we created a clear path for the customer to win the day, a plan that breaks through the fog that makes them want to take the next step and make a purchase? And then our calls to action clear, have we made it very clear what it is we want to want the customer to do, of course, to buy our product, if they're not ready to buy our product, to take the next step in a relationship? And then have we defined how our customer's life will improve if they do business with us? And also, have we identified the consequences we are helping our customers avoid? Those are the foundational questions of this sheet of paper, or if you're using the MyStoryBrand.com, the Digital Story Brand One Page. We want to be able to guide our customers or ourselves through our own company through this process in order to get the messages that we will use to populate all marketing from here on out. Listen, if you understand how to do this and you get really good at it, this is going to save you hours and hours and hours in your creation of great marketing collateral. You want to be able to fill out one of these Story Brand One Page sheets for your overall company, and then as you keep working with the company for each division of that company and for each product. But let's say somebody comes to you and says, Dom, we just have this big product launch coming up, we're releasing it. Then you don't want to create one for the company. Your job is going to be to create sales letters or wire frame a website for the release of this one product. Just do this for your own sanity. Create a Story Brand One Page. All right, I want to show you what marketing collateral can look like before and after a Story Brand One Page. This is Governor Jeb Bush's website a couple months before the South Carolina primary where he ended up dropping out of the presidential race. Bush had the most famous name probably in the entire race. He was picked to win the Republican nomination. But his website really wasn't very clear or pointed. He really felt like he needed to get his story out there and explain his story. But customers, voters simply couldn't find their story in his marketing collateral. Of course, it just says a governor's story. It has his name. It has the dates that he was governor. But what does that mean to me? I don't know. This is a story without a narrative that customers can engage. When you scroll down the website, it says something like, make it count. Well make what count? I don't know what you're talking about. And then there's two paragraphs that probably nobody has ever read. There's a video that probably very few people watched. And there's a countdown clock at the bottom. Now a countdown clock does create a sense of urgency, but really only does so in the last 48 hours. So with 343 days to go, his entire website is basically saying, here's a story about me. It's really not about anything very interesting, but there's no urgency in actually reading it, so don't worry about it. And the campaign numbers reflected that. He was at 3% when the campaign called us and asked us to step in and explain to them the story brand framework. We actually went into Miami and we gave Jeb a sample story brand one page. Now this is not the one that he used. It was a sample that we created in order to explain to the campaign manager and some key staff members how the process works. Again, it's not the campaign that he used, but he did change his messaging and saw an uptick based on what we presented. The basic message that we came up with is this. And this is the sort of work that you'll do on behalf of your clients or for your own company. We came up with the idea that Jeb would be clearing a path to the American dream. Now everybody can kind of find themselves in that story. If anybody feels stuck, they would find themselves in that story. What does the customer or the voter want? They want their shot at the American dream. What's their external problem? Inefficient bloated government. What's the internal problem? People feel stuck. The philosophical problem, it's wrong for a government to get in the way of its people. The external internal philosophical problem, just outlining it that way, gives me a great chance to do a stump speech. I know that you want your chance at the American dream, but the truth is you're not going to get it because of our inefficient bloated government. And I know you're feeling stuck behind this government. It feels like you're stuck in traffic. And let me tell you something. This is wrong. A government should not get in the way of its people. All I did was basically read verbatim the first two components of a Storybrand One page, and yet I've got a great speech started that people will pay attention to. That's how powerful a Storybrand One page can be. Then we introduced Jeb, and of course the two components of a guide are empathy and authority. And so we make the statement that Jeb understands why Americans feel stuck. And also the authority, the competence that he helped people get unstuck in Florida. He helped create an efficient government, and he can do it for the rest of America. Then Jeb would propose a three-step plan for making the government more efficient and getting it out of the way of the average sort of entrepreneurial conquering mindset of the average American, and so the government wouldn't block them. He would have a three-step plan. Of course, his real plan is going to be 250 million steps, but he needs to break it down into simple, easy-to-understand sound bites, and that's what you will do on behalf of your client or on behalf of your own company. Then he's got to call them to action, and he calls to action have to be very clear. You either going to donate, you're going to volunteer, or I want your vote. And because those calls to action are clear, nobody wonders what Jeb wants them to do. They're behind the story, they love the story, and now they can take action because he has clearly instructed them on how to take action. But of course, all this doesn't matter if life can end up better for me, if I do vote for you, and worse for me, if I don't. So if we do vote for Jeb, we get a strong, efficient government that helps rather than hinders all Americans, and if we don't vote for him, an inefficient government that gets larger under the leadership of Hillary Clinton, who we expected to win a Democratic nomination. It's just going to be four more years of bloated government. That is a very clear story brand one page. That's a clear campaign narrative. Whether you're running for president or selling dish soap, this is the sort of narrative we want because people can understand it, their brands can metabolize it, and they can respond to it, and they will respond to it. Then we want to take this story brand one page, and we want to create some great marketing collateral. We made this sample website on behalf of Jeb. It's not the one that they used, but they did use one that was pretty similar. And of course, right across the header of the website, clearing a path to the American Dream, and then you can read Jeb's plan. That's the transitional call to action. Now look at the paragraph at the bottom of this header. Everybody wants their shot at the American Dream. That's what, of course, the people want. But that dream has been blocked. That's the external problem. By an inefficient government, that's the villain. People are frustrated. That's the internal problem. Jeb understands. That's the empathy. And has a plan. That's, of course, the plan. He cleared the path in Florida. That's competency. That's authority. He'll clear the path for all Americans. That's success. The dream lives on. That's success. That's a comprehensive brand narrative in one short paragraph that people will actually read. Then we scroll down the page. We see Jeb's plan broken down into three steps. He's going to do these three things. And these three steps can be almost anything. They can be phases of his transition into a more efficient government. They can be the first three things he does when he gets into office, whatever. But it lifts the fog for people. It knows, okay, here's what I can expect if I vote for Jeb. When we included a transitional call to action, five ways the government is blocking your path through the American dream and how we can fix it. This is going to get a lot more people downloading that PDF than they would by signing up for Jeb's newsletter or anything like that. It's going to be a much more efficient and effective campaign. Why? Because we started with a story brand one page. In this campaign, the ambition is clear. The problem is serious. The guide is introduced. The plan will work. The calls to action are clear. The stakes are defined. It's a complete brand narrative people will understand and respond to. We want to be able to fill out a story brand one page for ourselves or on behalf of a client. And the more experience you have at this, the better you're going to do, the better you're going to get at this process. Because you'll be thinking as you fill out this one page, you'll already be thinking ahead to what that website's going to say and the different components of the website and the different kind of email nurturing campaigns you're going to create. You're going to be creating that one page for your client with the end in mind. That is, marketing collateral that gets a result. But it all starts with the story brand one page. It is the foundation of everything that you will do from here on out. So we've got to learn it. We've got to understand it. We've got to practice doing it. And we've got to get really good at it so that we can get results for ourselves or for our clients.
